items.passage: Many animals survive by blending into their surroundings through a remarkable adaptation called camouflage. Some lizards can change their skin color to match the rocks or sand around them. Predators are fooled because the prey seems to vanish against the background. Although camouflage works well, it can fail when an animal moves too quickly. Over many generations, the best-hidden creatures lived longer and passed these useful traits to their young.

Climate change describes the long-term shifts in temperature and weather patterns across the entire planet. Burning coal and oil releases gases that trap extra heat near the surface of the earth. As a result, glaciers are melting and the level of the ocean is slowly rising. Many regions now face stronger storms, longer droughts, and harvests that fail more often. Scientists warn that quick action is needed to protect both people and fragile natural systems.

Learning is the process through which the brain forms new connections and stores lasting memories. When a skill is practiced often, the links between brain cells grow stronger and faster. Reviewing material across several days works better than studying everything in one long session. Mistakes are valuable because they show the mind exactly where more effort is truly needed. Teachers often use rewards and clear goals to keep students curious and deeply motivated.

The ancient Silk Road linked distant civilizations and allowed cultural exchange to flourish for centuries. Traders carried silk, spices, and paper across vast deserts and high mountain passes. Along with goods, they spread new ideas, religions, and skills from one region to another. Although the journey was dangerous, the rewards drew merchants from many different lands. This steady flow of contact shaped languages, art, and beliefs in lasting and surprising ways.

Meteor impacts have shaped the surface of planets and moons throughout the long history of the solar system. When a large space rock strikes the ground, it releases enormous energy and carves a deep crater. The blast can throw rock and dust high into the air around the site. Some ancient impacts were so powerful that they changed the climate and wiped out many species. Geologists study these scars to learn how often such violent events occur.

Exoplanets are worlds that orbit stars far beyond our own solar system in distant space. Astronomers detect them by watching for the tiny dip in light when a planet crosses its star. Thousands of these planets have been found, and some lie within a zone where water could exist. A few may have rocky surfaces and mild temperatures much like those found on the earth. Future telescopes will search their atmospheres for chemical signs that life might be present.
